name: Lucky Millinder
abstract: Lucius Venable "Lucky" Millinder (August 8, 1910 – September 28, 1966) was an American swing and rhythm-and-blues bandleader. Although he could not read or write music, did not play an instrument and rarely sang, his showmanship and musical taste made his bands successful.

works.t: I Love You, Yes I Do

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Shaughnessy Part II
about_quotes.evidence: I’m playing with Lucky Millinder at the Savoy Ballroom in the early 50’s... I followed Art Blakey... Lucky looks up at me... I worked with that band, I traveled down south with that band
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Shaughnessy Part II interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 25, 1998 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder
about_quotes.evidence: Then I joined Lucky Millinder’s band. ... I used to be – when I was in the band for a time, I was the straw boss. I used to rehearse the band for him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Mitchell
about_quotes.evidence: I came to New York in Lucky Millinder’s band. 1948. I stayed in Lucky’s band two weeks, I got fired, and then I went back.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Mitchell interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Jacques Butler
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Lucky a long time. And then with Lucky Millinder

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: So they said, “Lucky Millinder’s looking for a guitar player. He’s breaking up the Blue Rhythm Band. It’s going to be Lucky Millinder and his Cotton Club Orchestra. No more Blue Rhythm Band.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Danny Barker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: So I got a job with Lucky Millinder. Lucky Millinder had a very good band, a great band. However, I stayed there, I think it was three weeks and I got fired
